Enter THESEUS, HIPPOLYTA, PHILOSTRATE, and Attendants

2.play: THESEUS: Now, fair Hippolyta, our nuptial hour

3.play: Draws on apace; four happy days bring in

4.play: Another moon: but, O, methinks, how slow

5.play: This old moon wanes! she lingers my desires,

6.play: Like to a step-dame or a dowager

7.play: Long withering out a young man revenue.

8.play: HIPPOLYTA: Four days will quickly steep themselves in night;

9.play: Four nights will quickly dream away the time;

10.play: And then the moon, like to a silver bow

11.play: New-bent in heaven, shall behold the night

14.play: Stir up the Athenian youth to merriments;

15.play: Awake the pert and nimble spirit of mirth;

17.play: The pale companion is not for our pomp.

18.play: Hippolyta, I woo'd thee with my sword,

19.play: And won thy love, doing thee injuries;

20.play: But I will wed thee in another key,

21.play: With pomp, with triumph and with revelling.

22.play: EGEUS: Happy be Theseus, our renowned duke!

23.play: THESEUS: Thanks, good Egeus: what's the news with thee?

24.play: EGEUS: Full of vexation come I, with complaint

25.play: Against my child, my daughter Hermia.

26.play: Stand forth, Demetrius. My noble lord,

27.play: This man hath my consent to marry her.

28.play: Stand forth, Lysander: and my gracious duke,

29.play: This man hath bewitch'd the bosom of my child;

30.play: Thou, thou, Lysander, thou hast given her rhymes,

31.play: And interchanged love-tokens with my child:

32.play: Thou hast by moonlight at her window sung,

33.play: With feigning voice verses of feigning love,

34.play: And stolen the impression of her fantasy

35.play: With bracelets of thy hair, rings, gawds, conceits,

37.play: Of strong prevailment in unharden'd youth:

38.play: With cunning hast thou filch'd my daughter's heart,

39.play: Turn'd her obedience, which is due to me,

40.play: To stubborn harshness: and, my gracious duke,

41.play: Be it so she; will not here before your grace

43.play: I beg the ancient privilege of Athens,

44.play: As she is mine, I may dispose of her:

45.play: Which shall be either to this gentleman

46.play: Or to her death, according to our law

48.play: THESEUS: What say you, Hermia? be advised fair maid:

49.play: To you your father should be as a god;

50.play: One that composed your beauties, yea, and one

51.play: To whom you are but as a form in wax

52.play: By him imprinted and within his power

53.play: To leave the figure or disfigure it.

57.play: But in this kind, wanting your father's voice,

58.play: The other must be held the worthier.

59.play: HERMIA: I would my father look'd but with my eyes.

60.play: THESEUS: Rather your eyes must with his judgment look.

61.play: HERMIA: I do entreat your grace to pardon me.

62.play: I know not by what power I am made bold,

63.play: Nor how it may concern my modesty,

64.play: In such a presence here to plead my thoughts;

65.play: But I beseech your grace that I may know

66.play: The worst that may befall me in this case,

67.play: If I refuse to wed Demetrius.

68.play: THESEUS: Either to die the death or to abjure

69.play: For ever the society of men.

70.play: Therefore, fair Hermia, question your desires;

71.play: Know of your youth, examine well your blood,

72.play: Whether, if you yield not to your father's choice,

73.play: You can endure the livery of a nun,

74.play: For aye to be in shady cloister mew'd,

75.play: To live a barren sister all your life,

76.play: Chanting faint hymns to the cold fruitless moon.

77.play: Thrice-blessed they that master so their blood,

79.play: But earthlier happy is the rose distill'd,

80.play: Than that which withering on the virgin thorn

81.play: Grows, lives and dies in single blessedness.

82.play: HERMIA: So will I grow, so live, so die, my lord,

83.play: Ere I will my virgin patent up

84.play: Unto his lordship, whose unwished yoke

85.play: My soul consents not to give sovereignty.

86.play: THESEUS: Take time to pause; and, by the next new moon--

87.play: The sealing-day betwixt my love and me,

89.play: Upon that day either prepare to die

90.play: For disobedience to your father's will,

91.play: Or else to wed Demetrius, as he would;

92.play: Or on Diana's altar to protest

93.play: For aye austerity and single life.

94.play: DEMETRIUS: Relent, sweet Hermia: and, Lysander, yield

95.play: Thy crazed title to my certain right.

96.play: LYSANDER: You have her father's love, Demetrius;

97.play: Let me have Hermia's: do you marry him.

98.play: EGEUS: Scornful Lysander! true, he hath my love,

99.play: And what is mine my love shall render him.

100.play: And she is mine, and all my right of her

102.play: LYSANDER: I am, my lord, as well derived as he,

103.play: As well possess'd; my love is more than his;

104.play: My fortunes every way as fairly rank'd,

105.play: If not with vantage, as Demetrius';

106.play: And, which is more than all these boasts can be,

107.play: I am beloved of beauteous Hermia:

108.play: Why should not I then prosecute my right?

109.play: Demetrius, I'll avouch it to his head,

110.play: Made love to Nedar's daughter, Helena,

111.play: And won her soul; and she, sweet lady, dotes,

113.play: Upon this spotted and inconstant man.

114.play: THESEUS: I must confess that I have heard so much,

115.play: And with Demetrius thought to have spoke thereof;

117.play: My mind did lose it. But, Demetrius, come;

118.play: And come, Egeus; you shall go with me,

119.play: I have some private schooling for you both.

120.play: For you, fair Hermia, look you arm yourself

121.play: To fit your fancies to your father's will;

122.play: Or else the law of Athens yields you up--

123.play: Which by no means we may extenuate--

124.play: To death, or to a vow of single life.

125.play: Come, my Hippolyta: what cheer, my love?

127.play: I must employ you in some business

128.play: Against our nuptial and confer with you

129.play: Of something nearly that concerns yourselves.

130.play: EGEUS: With duty and desire we follow you.

131.play: LYSANDER: How now, my love! why is your cheek so pale?

132.play: How chance the roses there do fade so fast?

133.play: HERMIA: Belike for want of rain, which I could well

134.play: Beteem them from the tempest of my eyes.

135.play: LYSANDER: Ay me! for aught that I could ever read,

136.play: Could ever hear by tale or history,

137.play: The course of true love never did run smooth;

138.play: But, either it was different in blood,--

139.play: HERMIA: O cross! too high to be enthrall'd to low.

140.play: LYSANDER: Or else misgraffed in respect of years,--

141.play: HERMIA: O spite! too old to be engaged to young.

142.play: LYSANDER: Or else it stood upon the choice of friends,--

143.play: HERMIA: O hell! to choose love by another's eyes.

144.play: LYSANDER: Or, if there were a sympathy in choice,

145.play: War, death, or sickness did lay siege to it,

146.play: Making it momentany as a sound,

147.play: Swift as a shadow, short as any dream;

148.play: Brief as the lightning in the collied night,

149.play: That, in a spleen, unfolds both heaven and earth,

150.play: And ere a man hath power to say 'Behold!'

151.play: The jaws of darkness do devour it up:

152.play: So quick bright things come to confusion.

153.play: HERMIA: If then true lovers have been ever cross'd,

154.play: It stands as an edict in destiny:

155.play: Then let us teach our trial patience,

156.play: Because it is a customary cross,

157.play: As due to love as thoughts and dreams and sighs,

158.play: Wishes and tears, poor fancy's followers.

159.play: LYSANDER: A good persuasion: therefore, hear me, Hermia.

160.play: I have a widow aunt, a dowager

161.play: Of great revenue, and she hath no child:

162.play: From Athens is her house remote seven leagues;

163.play: And she respects me as her only son.

164.play: There, gentle Hermia, may I marry thee;

165.play: And to that place the sharp Athenian law

166.play: Cannot pursue us. If thou lovest me then,

167.play: Steal forth thy father's house to-morrow night;

168.play: And in the wood, a league without the town,

169.play: Where I did meet thee once with Helena,

170.play: To do observance to a morn of May,

171.play: There will I stay for thee.

173.play: I swear to thee, by Cupid's strongest bow,

174.play: By his best arrow with the golden head,

175.play: By the simplicity of Venus' doves,

176.play: By that which knitteth souls and prospers loves,

177.play: And by that fire which burn'd the Carthage queen,

178.play: When the false Troyan under sail was seen,

179.play: By all the vows that ever men have broke,

180.play: In number more than ever women spoke,

181.play: In that same place thou hast appointed me,

182.play: To-morrow truly will I meet with thee.

183.play: LYSANDER: Keep promise, love. Look, here comes Helena.

184.play: HERMIA: God speed fair Helena! whither away?

185.play: HELENA: Call you me fair? that fair again unsay.

186.play: Demetrius loves your fair: O happy fair!

187.play: Your eyes are lode-stars; and your tongue's sweet air

188.play: More tuneable than lark to shepherd's ear,

189.play: When wheat is green, when hawthorn buds appear.

190.play: Sickness is catching: O, were favour so,

191.play: Yours would I catch, fair Hermia, ere I go;

192.play: My ear should catch your voice, my eye your eye,

193.play: My tongue should catch your tongue's sweet melody.

194.play: Were the world mine, Demetrius being bated,

195.play: The rest I'd give to be to you translated.

196.play: O, teach me how you look, and with what art

197.play: You sway the motion of Demetrius' heart.

198.play: HERMIA: I frown upon him, yet he loves me still.

199.play: HELENA: O that your frowns would teach my smiles such skill!

200.play: HERMIA: I give him curses, yet he gives me love.

201.play: HELENA: O that my prayers could such affection move!

202.play: HERMIA: The more I hate, the more he follows me.

203.play: HELENA: The more I love, the more he hateth me.

204.play: HERMIA: His folly, Helena, is no fault of mine.

205.play: HELENA: None, but your beauty: would that fault were mine!

206.play: HERMIA: Take comfort: he no more shall see my face;

207.play: Lysander and myself will fly this place.

208.play: Before the time I did Lysander see,

209.play: Seem'd Athens as a paradise to me:

210.play: O, then, what graces in my love do dwell,

211.play: That he hath turn'd a heaven unto a hell!

212.play: LYSANDER: Helen, to you our minds we will unfold:

213.play: To-morrow night, when Phoebe doth behold

214.play: Her silver visage in the watery glass,

215.play: Decking with liquid pearl the bladed grass,

216.play: A time that lovers' flights doth still conceal,

217.play: Through Athens' gates have we devised to steal.

218.play: HERMIA: And in the wood, where often you and I

219.play: Upon faint primrose-beds were wont to lie,

220.play: Emptying our bosoms of their counsel sweet,

221.play: There my Lysander and myself shall meet;

222.play: And thence from Athens turn away our eyes,

223.play: To seek new friends and stranger companies.

224.play: Farewell, sweet playfellow: pray thou for us;

225.play: And good luck grant thee thy Demetrius!

226.play: Keep word, Lysander: we must starve our sight

227.play: From lovers' food till morrow deep midnight.

230.play: As you on him, Demetrius dote on you!

231.play: HELENA: How happy some o'er other some can be!

232.play: Through Athens I am thought as fair as she.

233.play: But what of that? Demetrius thinks not so;

234.play: He will not know what all but he do know:

235.play: And as he errs, doting on Hermia's eyes,

236.play: So I, admiring of his qualities:

237.play: Things base and vile, folding no quantity,

238.play: Love can transpose to form and dignity:

239.play: Love looks not with the eyes, but with the mind;

240.play: And therefore is wing'd Cupid painted blind:

241.play: Nor hath Love's mind of any judgement taste;

242.play: Wings and no eyes figure unheedy haste:

243.play: And therefore is Love said to be a child,

244.play: Because in choice he is so oft beguiled.

245.play: As waggish boys in game themselves forswear,

246.play: So the boy Love is perjured every where:

247.play: For ere Demetrius look'd on Hermia's eyne,

248.play: He hail'd down oaths that he was only mine;

249.play: And when this hail some heat from Hermia felt,

250.play: So he dissolved, and showers of oaths did melt.

251.play: I will go tell him of fair Hermia's flight:

252.play: Then to the wood will he to-morrow night

253.play: Pursue her; and for this intelligence

254.play: If I have thanks, it is a dear expense:

255.play: But herein mean I to enrich my pain,

256.play: To have his sight thither and back again.

3.play: BOTTOM: You were best to call them generally, man by man,

12.play: QUINCE: Marry, our play is, The most lamentable comedy, and

13.play: most cruel death of Pyramus and Thisby.

14.play: BOTTOM: A very good piece of work, I assure you, and a

18.play: BOTTOM: Ready. Name what part I am for, and proceed.

19.play: QUINCE: You, Nick Bottom, are set down for Pyramus.

20.play: BOTTOM: What is Pyramus? a lover, or a tyrant?

21.play: QUINCE: A lover, that kills himself most gallant for love.

22.play: BOTTOM: That will ask some tears in the true performing of

27.play: tear a cat in, to make all split.

36.play: This was lofty! Now name the rest of the players.

37.play: This is Ercles' vein, a tyrant's vein; a lover is

41.play: QUINCE: Flute, you must take Thisby on you.

42.play: FLUTE: What is Thisby? a wandering knight?

43.play: QUINCE: It is the lady that Pyramus must love.

44.play: FLUTE: Nay, faith, let me not play a woman; I have a beard coming.

45.play: QUINCE: That's all one: you shall play it in a mask, and

46.play: you may speak as small as you will.

47.play: BOTTOM: An I may hide my face, let me play Thisby too, I'll

48.play: speak in a monstrous little voice. 'Thisne,

49.play: Thisne;' 'Ah, Pyramus, lover dear! thy Thisby dear,

51.play: QUINCE: No, no; you must play Pyramus: and, Flute, you Thisby.

55.play: QUINCE: Robin Starveling, you must play Thisby's mother.

58.play: QUINCE: You, Pyramus' father: myself, Thisby's father:

59.play: Snug, the joiner; you, the lion's part: and, I

60.play: hope, here is a play fitted.

61.play: SNUG: Have you the lion's part written? pray you, if it

62.play: be, give it me, for I am slow of study.

63.play: QUINCE: You may do it extempore, for it is nothing but roaring.

64.play: BOTTOM: Let me play the lion too: I will roar, that I will

65.play: do any man's heart good to hear me; I will roar,

66.play: that I will make the duke say 'Let him roar again,

68.play: QUINCE: An you should do it too terribly, you would fright

70.play: and that were enough to hang us all.

71.play: ALL: That would hang us, every mother's son.

72.play: BOTTOM: I grant you, friends, if that you should fright the

73.play: ladies out of their wits, they would have no more

74.play: discretion but to hang us: but I will aggravate my

75.play: voice so that I will roar you as gently as any

78.play: QUINCE: You can play no part but Pyramus; for Pyramus is a

79.play: sweet-faced man; a proper man, as one shall see in a

80.play: summer's day; a most lovely gentleman-like man:

81.play: therefore you must needs play Pyramus.

82.play: BOTTOM: Well, I will undertake it. What beard were I best

86.play: beard, your orange-tawny beard, your purple-in-grain

89.play: QUINCE: Some of your French crowns have no hair at all, and

1.play: Enter, from opposite sides, a Fairy, and PUCK

2.play: PUCK: How now, spirit! whither wander you?

3.play: The king doth keep his revels here to-night:

4.play: Take heed the queen come not within his sight;

5.play: For Oberon is passing fell and wrath,

6.play: Because that she as her attendant hath

7.play: A lovely boy, stolen from an Indian king;

8.play: She never had so sweet a changeling;

9.play: And jealous Oberon would have the child

10.play: Knight of his train, to trace the forests wild;

11.play: But she perforce withholds the loved boy,

12.play: Crowns him with flowers and makes him all her joy:

13.play: And now they never meet in grove or green,

14.play: By fountain clear, or spangled starlight sheen,

15.play: But, they do square, that all their elves for fear

16.play: Creep into acorn-cups and hide them there.

18.play: I am that merry wanderer of the night.

19.play: I jest to Oberon and make him smile

20.play: When I a fat and bean-fed horse beguile,

21.play: Neighing in likeness of a filly foal:

22.play: And sometime lurk I in a gossip's bowl,

23.play: In very likeness of a roasted crab,

24.play: And when she drinks, against her lips I bob

25.play: And on her wither'd dewlap pour the ale.

26.play: The wisest aunt, telling the saddest tale,

27.play: Sometime for three-foot stool mistaketh me;

28.play: Then slip I from her bum, down topples she,

29.play: And 'tailor' cries, and falls into a cough;

30.play: And then the whole quire hold their hips and laugh,

31.play: And waxen in their mirth and neeze and swear

32.play: A merrier hour was never wasted there.

33.play: But, room, fairy! here comes Oberon.

34.play: Enter, from one side, OBERON, with his train; from the other, TITANIA, with hers

35.play: OBERON: Ill met by moonlight, proud Titania.

36.play: TITANIA: What, jealous Oberon! Fairies, skip hence:

37.play: I have forsworn his bed and company.

38.play: OBERON: Tarry, rash wanton: am not I thy lord?

39.play: TITANIA: Then I must be thy lady: but I know

40.play: When thou hast stolen away from fairy land,

41.play: And in the shape of Corin sat all day,

42.play: Playing on pipes of corn and versing love

43.play: To amorous Phillida. Why art thou here,

44.play: Come from the farthest Steppe of India?

45.play: But that, forsooth, the bouncing Amazon,

46.play: Your buskin'd mistress and your warrior love,

47.play: To Theseus must be wedded, and you come

48.play: To give their bed joy and prosperity.

49.play: OBERON: How canst thou thus for shame, Titania,

50.play: Glance at my credit with Hippolyta,

51.play: Knowing I know thy love to Theseus?

52.play: Didst thou not lead him through the glimmering night

54.play: And make him with fair AEgle break his faith,

56.play: TITANIA: These are the forgeries of jealousy:

57.play: And never, since the middle summer's spring,

58.play: Met we on hill, in dale, forest or mead,

59.play: By paved fountain or by rushy brook,

60.play: Or in the beached margent of the sea,

61.play: To dance our ringlets to the whistling wind,

62.play: But with thy brawls thou hast disturb'd our sport.

63.play: Therefore the winds, piping to us in vain,

64.play: As in revenge, have suck'd up from the sea

65.play: Contagious fogs; which falling in the land

66.play: Have every pelting river made so proud

67.play: That they have overborne their continents:

68.play: The ox hath therefore stretch'd his yoke in vain,

69.play: The ploughman lost his sweat, and the green corn

70.play: Hath rotted ere his youth attain'd a beard;

71.play: The fold stands empty in the drowned field,

72.play: And crows are fatted with the murrion flock;

73.play: The nine men's morris is fill'd up with mud,

74.play: And the quaint mazes in the wanton green

75.play: For lack of tread are undistinguishable:

76.play: The human mortals want their winter here;

77.play: No night is now with hymn or carol blest:

78.play: Therefore the moon, the governess of floods,

79.play: Pale in her anger, washes all the air,

81.play: And thorough this distemperature we see

83.play: Far in the fresh lap of the crimson rose,

84.play: And on old Hiems' thin and icy crown

85.play: An odorous chaplet of sweet summer buds

86.play: Is, as in mockery, set: the spring, the summer,

87.play: The childing autumn, angry winter, change

88.play: Their wonted liveries, and the mazed world,

89.play: By their increase, now knows not which is which:

90.play: And this same progeny of evils comes

91.play: From our debate, from our dissension;

92.play: We are their parents and original.

93.play: OBERON: Do you amend it then; it lies in you:

94.play: Why should Titania cross her Oberon?

95.play: I do but beg a little changeling boy,

97.play: TITANIA: Set your heart at rest:

98.play: The fairy land buys not the child of me.

99.play: His mother was a votaress of my order:

100.play: And, in the spiced Indian air, by night,

101.play: Full often hath she gossip'd by my side,

102.play: And sat with me on Neptune's yellow sands,

103.play: Marking the embarked traders on the flood,

104.play: When we have laugh'd to see the sails conceive

105.play: And grow big-bellied with the wanton wind;

106.play: Which she, with pretty and with swimming gait

107.play: Following,--her womb then rich with my young squire,--

108.play: Would imitate, and sail upon the land,

109.play: To fetch me trifles, and return again,

110.play: As from a voyage, rich with merchandise.

111.play: But she, being mortal, of that boy did die;

112.play: And for her sake do I rear up her boy,

113.play: And for her sake I will not part with him.

114.play: OBERON: How long within this wood intend you stay?

115.play: TITANIA: Perchance till after Theseus' wedding-day.

116.play: If you will patiently dance in our round

117.play: And see our moonlight revels, go with us;

118.play: If not, shun me, and I will spare your haunts.

119.play: OBERON: Give me that boy, and I will go with thee.

120.play: TITANIA: Not for thy fairy kingdom. Fairies, away!

121.play: We shall chide downright, if I longer stay.

122.play: OBERON: Well, go thy way: thou shalt not from this grove

123.play: Till I torment thee for this injury.

124.play: My gentle Puck, come hither. Thou rememberest

125.play: Since once I sat upon a promontory,

126.play: And heard a mermaid on a dolphin's back

127.play: Uttering such dulcet and harmonious breath

128.play: That the rude sea grew civil at her song

129.play: And certain stars shot madly from their spheres,

132.play: OBERON: That very time I saw, but thou couldst not,

133.play: Flying between the cold moon and the earth,

134.play: Cupid all arm'd: a certain aim he took

135.play: At a fair vestal throned by the west,

136.play: And loosed his love-shaft smartly from his bow,

137.play: As it should pierce a hundred thousand hearts;

138.play: But I might see young Cupid's fiery shaft

139.play: Quench'd in the chaste beams of the watery moon,

244.play: Wink each at other; hold the sweet jest up:
